title: Turing Award Winner: TPUs vs GPUs vs CPUs, Computer Architecture, RISC vs CISC | David Patterson
guest_role: Turing Award winner, UC Berkeley professor, RISC pioneer, Google distinguished engineer

sections.title: How to have a bad career

key_takeaways: The 1980s RISC vs CISC debate was ferocious partly because architecture lacked quantitative methods; once measured, RISC needed ~30-40% more instructions but ran them 4-5x faster, a net 3-4x potential speedup.

RISC effectively won: ARM (originally Acorn RISC Machine, renamed when Apple used it in the Newton) has shipped ~350 billion processors and ~99% of processors today are RISC; x86 survived by translating its instructions into RISC micro-ops in hardware, worth the overhead because of the PC binary software base.

Compilers were central to the RISC argument: as register allocation improved, simple instructions plus 32 registers beat sophisticated CISC instructions that compiler writers refused to use, making the microcode interpreter pure overhead.

Dennard scaling ended around 2005 (chips could no longer stay at tens of watts), forcing multicore; then Moore's Law slowed in the 2010s, forcing the ~2015 turn to domain-specific architectures just as machine learning burst on the scene as the obvious target domain.
AlexNet's 2012 win — by a Toronto student who had taken a CUDA course — flipped the whole field to neural networks on GPUs within a few years.

Google's 2016 TPU was the watershed: clean-slate design around a giant matrix-multiply unit, software-managed memory instead of caches, and bfloat16 (first format with exponent bigger than fraction), achieving ~30x GPU and ~80x CPU inference performance and forcing Intel and NVIDIA to respond.
NVIDIA's moat is the libraries as much as CUDA: armies of engineers re-tailor libraries for each architecture, which is also why startups struggle on MLPerf and the benchmark is less universally run.

Moore's Law is genuinely over (SRAM barely improves; DRAM density gains slowed from 4x/3yr to ~10yr per factor); progress now comes from chiplet/reticle packaging, 8-bit and 4-bit floating point, and bigger matrix units — and denial is partly emotional since careers were built on sustaining Moore's Law.

Career advice: keep family first, optimize happiness over wealth, protect time for important non-urgent work (Covey quadrant), and finish things — you're remembered for the 5-6 things you complete, not hundreds you start.

Courage is career-critical — fortune favors the bold, and someone should stand up to weak arguments even from leaders — but 'friends come and go, enemies accumulate.' His one regret: not knowing about harassment at SIGARCH conferences he chaired, which he would have stopped.

notable_quotes.speaker: David Patterson
notable_quotes.quote: It turned out you needed maybe 30%, 40% more simple instructions to execute in a program. But you could run them 4 or 5 times faster. So the net was 3 or 4x speedup was the potential for RISC over CISC.

notable_quotes.speaker: David Patterson
notable_quotes.quote: Nobody on their deathbed says, you know, I wish I spent more time in the office.

notable_quotes.speaker: David Patterson
notable_quotes.quote: It's not how many things you start, it's how many things you finish.

notable_quotes.speaker: David Patterson
notable_quotes.quote: We boiled it down to nine magic words... I was wrong, you were right. I love you.
